text: I would like to thank the committee for allowing us to present our concurrent robotic APR and prostatectomy. This is a sixty-one-year-old man who presented with three weeks of rectal bleeding, anemia, and perianal pain. He had a medical history significant for prostate cancer. This was diagnosed four years ago, and at the time, prostatectomy and radiation were recommended. However, the patient chose holistic therapy.
The video shows the port placement for the robotic ampullectomy procedure, with ports strategically positioned for optimal access to the rectal tumor starting at the dentate line.
The surgical team identifies the ureters bilaterally, with black dashes marking the eight-millimeter robotic ports and a red dash indicating the laparoscopic assist port.

The surgeon dissects the mesorectum, using a Ligasure device to take the lateral stalks. The dissection continues on the right side, incising the peritoneum over the right lateral mesorectum to enter the presacral space. The ureter on the right is identified, and the mesentery is dissected to locate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The mesentery around the IMA is thinned using electrocautery and Ligasure, and the IMA is stapled with an Endo GIA stapler vascular load.

The surgeon observes the significantly redundant colon, with the descending colon already mobilized along the white line of Toldt. Dissection continues through the mesentery, preparing the colon for division with a stapler. After completing the prostatectomy, the bowel is transected laparoscopically. The remainder of the abdominoperineal resection (APR) is performed from the perineum in the prone jackknife position.

The surgeon begins the prostatectomy by dissecting the vas deferens and seminal vesicles, developing the preperitoneal space. The dissection proceeds through the space of Retzius, and the dorsal venous complex is divided using a stapler. A right indirect inguinal hernia is noted during the procedure.

The surgeon transects the neck of the bladder using electrocautery, then passes the Foley catheter through the prosthetic urethra to retract the prostate anteriorly. The dissection continues posteriorly, dividing the posterior prostatic fascia to expose the seminal vesicles, vas deferens, and lateral prostatic pedicles. The lateral pedicles are taken with the ligasure, and the prostate is dissected free from the remaining periprosthetic fascia. The neurovascular bundle is preserved laterally. The Foley catheter is pulled back, and the urethra is transected distal to the prostate, concluding the prostatectomy portion of the operation.

The surgeon places the prostate in an endocatch bag behind the rectum to begin the urethra vesicle anastomosis. The anastomosis is initiated posteriorly, with the Foley catheter intermittently inserted and withdrawn to identify the urethral lumen and ensure mucosal inclusion in each full-thickness suture bite. The anastomosis is completed and secured with a locking suture clip.

The surgeon divides the bowel laparoscopically and completes the abdominoperineal resection (APR) from the perineum. The prostate is extracted through the perineal wound. An ostomy is matured at the left lower quadrant site marked by the ostomy nurse. The patient is positioned on his right side.
